coredata.dc:title: Charge storage of carbon dot enhances photo-production of H2 and H2O2 over Ni2P/carbon dot catalyst under normal pressure
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yan
Zhao, Yajie
Wu, Qingyao
Wang, Xiao
Nie, Haodong
Zhou, Yunjie
Huang, Hui
Shao, Mingwang
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Photo-production of hydrogen (H2) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) simultaneously from water catalytic-splitting has received widespread attention. Most photocatalysts suffer from H2O2 poisoning and rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Although photocatalysts against H2O2 poisoning have been prepared, the long-time stability of photogenerated electrons is still a great challenge. Here, we demonstrate that carbon dots (CDs) can stabilize and store the photogenerated electrons, promote the effective separation of photo-generated charges, and improve the photocatalytic activity and stability of the catalyst. The Ni2P/CDs composite photocatalyst synthesized in this work can produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously under atmosphere, and the yields of H2 and H2O2 can reach 258.6 μmol/h/g and 1281.4 μmol/h/g, respectively. It represents the highest yield of H2O2 ever reported in photocatalysts capable of producing both H2 and H2O2 simultaneously under normal pressure. This work provides some deep insight on the storage and stability of photogenerated electrons, and open up a new way for the high efficient photocatalyst design based on the regulation of the interface charge dynamics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
Hydrogen peroxide production
Electron storage
Normal pressure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China
National Key Research and Development Program of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication and morphology of Ni2P/CDs composites. (a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Ni2P/CDs. (b) SEM, (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of Ni2P/CDs. (e) STEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping images of (f) Ni (Ni-K, yellow), (g) P (P-K, red), (h) C (C-K, green) of the Ni2P/CDs (scale bar: 50 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Structure and property of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs. (a) Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni2P/CDs: (b)–(d) Ni 2p, P 2p, and C 1s. (e) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve of Ni2P/CDs. (f) Band structure diagram of Ni2P/CDs.

Transient photovoltage (TPV) patterns of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs. (a) TPV patterns of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs. (b) The enlarged view of the tmax (the time required for peak intensity to reach maximum) in TPV curves of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs. (c) The enlarged view of the charge extraction process in TPV curves and the integration of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs. (d) The fitting curve of charge attenuation process in TPV curves of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs.

In-situ transient photovoltage tests of Ni2P/CDs. (a) In-situ transient photovoltage (TPV) patterns of Ni2P/CDs under different conditions. (b) The fitting curve of charge attenuation process in in-situ TPV curves of Ni2P/CDs under different conditions. (c) The in-situ TPV curves and integration of Ni2P/CDs under N2-saturated acetonitrile and 0.5 vol% water/acetonitrile. (d) The in-situ TPV patterns of Ni2P/CDs under N2-saturated acetonitrile and O2-saturated acetonitrile.

H2 and H2O2 photo-production over Ni2P/CDs (10 mg catalyst, 20 mL deionized water). (a) The amount of H2 and H2O2 produced by photocatalysts with different CDs contents in saturated air. (b) The curve of H2 and H2O2 production over time. (c) The cyclic stability of Ni2P/CD as catalyst for H2 and H2O2 evolution in saturated air atmosphere. (d) The rate ratio of H2 and H2O2 produced by photocatalysts with different CDs contents in air. (e) The amount of H2 and H2O2 produced by Ni2P/CDs in different atmospheres. (f) The quantum efficiency of Ni2P/CDs at different wavelengths in saturated O2 atmosphere.

Photo-electrochemical test for photocatalytic reaction mechanism. (a) Photocurrent curves over time and (b) EIS Nyquist plots of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs. (c) ORR LSV and (d) HER LSV curves in 0.1 M Na2SO4 for CDs and Ni2P.
The mechanism diagram of H2 and H2O2 production over Ni2P/CDs photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Charge storage of carbon dot enhances photo-production of H2 and H2O2 over Ni2P/carbon dot catalyst under normal pressure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-production of hydrogen (H2) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) simultaneously from water catalytic-splitting has received widespread attention. Most photocatalysts suffer from H2O2 poisoning and rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Although photocatalysts against H2O2 poisoning have been prepared, the long-time stability of photogenerated electrons is still a great challenge. Here, we demonstrate that carbon dots (CDs) can stabilize and store the photogenerated electrons, promote the effective separation of photo-generated charges, and improve the photocatalytic activity and stability of the catalyst. The Ni2P/CDs composite photocatalyst synthesized in this work can produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously under atmosphere, and the yields of H2 and H2O2 can reach 258.6 μmol/h/g and 1281.4 μmol/h/g, respectively. It represents the highest yield of H2O2 ever reported in photocatalysts capable of producing both H2 and H2O2 simultaneously under normal pressure. This work provides some deep insight on the storage and stability of photogenerated electrons, and open up a new way for the high efficient photocatalyst design based on the regulation of the interface charge dynamics.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2), a green and pollution-free renewable energy, has become one of the candidate energy sources. The generation of H2 through photocatalytic water splitting is considered to be the lowest energy consumption way [1–7]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is considered as an ideal alternative to traditional fuels due to its wide industrial value [8–11], but the traditional production method (anthraquinone process) of hydrogen peroxide is accompanied by high cost and potential safety problems. Undoubtedly, photocatalytic decomposition of water is the cleanest and optimum way to produce H2 and H2O2. Although more and more photocatalysts have been explored to have the ability to generate H2 and H2O2 by photocatalysis, the poisoning of H2O2, the rapid recombination of photo-generated charges and the assistance of sacrificial agents greatly limit the development of catalysts [12–15]. The latest research progress shows that some photocatalyst can produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously under normal pressure. However, the rapid recombination of photo-generated charges leads to the low catalytic activity of the catalyst [16,17].

Carbon materials, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots and so on, are widely used in the field of catalysis due to their excellent photocatalytic and electrocatalytic properties [18–22]. Notably, due to unique optical and electrical properties, carbon dots (CDs) have been demonstrated to significantly enhance the light absorption performance and charge separation efficiency in the composite photocatalysis, thereby improving the catalytic activity and stability of photocatalysts [23–27]. Here, Ni2P is chosen as combined with CDs to prepare photocatalyst Ni2P/CDs, which has about 2.5 times higher H2O2 generation activity than that of CoP/CDs (our recent reported photocatalyst) and has a lower price [17]. In this catalytic system, O2 is produced by oxidation of water through a 4e− process, while the H2 or H2O2 is produced by a 2e− pathway. Notably, CDs have unique characteristics of storing electrons, which can accelerate the separation of photogenerated charges, stabilize photogenerated electrons, and improve photocatalytic activity. The maximum yields of H2 and H2O2 can reach up to 258.6 μmol/h/g and 1281.4 μmol/h/g, respectively, which represents the highest yield of H2O2 ever reported in the photocatalysts produced H2 and H2O2 simultaneously. Further, by combining electrochemical tests and transient photovoltage (TPV) tests, the effect of the adding CDs on promoting charge separation and improving catalytic activity and stability has been clearly revealed.

The synthesis of CDs was based on our previous report3. In brief, two graphite rods as electrodes were placed in ultrapure water and then applied at 30 V voltage. Over time, the graphite rods gradually corroded, and the water gradually turned brown. Then, the brown solution containing CDs was filtered and freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder for standby use.

Typically, 0.992 g urea, 1.298 g NiCl2·6H2O, 300 mL ultra-pure water and CDs powder of different weights (0, 10, 20, 30, 40 mg) were added to a beaker. The mixed solution was first stirred for 30 min at room temperature, then divided into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL), heated at 150 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the precursor was washed three times with water and ethanol, respectively, and the product was dried at 70 °C under vacuum conditions. Next, the precursor of the obtained composite was further phosphated in a tubular furnace. The precursor and sodium hypophosphite were laid on both side of the porcelain boat at the molar ratio of Ni/P = 1:10, and sodium hypophosphite was placed on the upstream side, then heated at 300 °C under saturated nitrogen (N2) atmosphere for 120 min. Finally, the resulting powder was washed three times with water and ethanol, respectively, and dried at 70 °C under vacuum conditions. The composites with different CDs contents were marked as NPC-0, NPC-10, NPC-20, NPC-30 and NPC-40, respectively.

A multi-channel photocatalytic reactor was applied to measure the photocatalytic performance of the samples (PCX50B, Beijing Perfectlight Co. Ltd, China) under visible light (λ＞420 nm), without adding any sacrificial agents and co-catalysts. Typically, catalyst (10 mg), ultrapure water (20 mL) and magnet were added to a photoreaction vial (50 mL) and irradiated with visible light at atmospheric pressure for 8 h. Besides, the production of H2 and H2O2 need to be measured in different atmospheres. The suspension after the reaction was centrifuged and dried to test catalytic stability. Gas chromatograph (GC-7900T, 5A molecular sieve column, TCD detector) is used to detect the generated H2. Likewise, the output of H2O2 was determined by potassium permanganate titration. Firstly, the solution after reaction was centrifuged, then the supernatant was filtered and titrated with acidic potassium permanganate solution until the solution was discolored within half a minute. The amount of hydrogen peroxide produced in the solution can be calculated by the amount of potassium permanganate consumed.

A schematic diagram of the synthetic route for Ni2P/CDs is shown in Fig. 1 a. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) explore the morphology and microstructure of the synthesized samples. It could be observed in Fig. 1b the morphology of Ni2P/CDs is a micron flower composed of a large number of fragments. The SEM image of Ni2P displayed in Fig. S1 illustrates that the CDs can modulate the morphology of Ni2P. Moreover, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms in Fig. S2 depict the Ni2P/CDs has a larger specific surface area than that of Ni2P. More detailed information on the structure for Ni2P and CDs is observed by TEM and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1c and d). Namely, the interplanar spacing of 0.22 and 0.21 nm correspond to the (111) plane of Ni2P and (100) plane of CDs [28–31]. The mapping images in Fig. 1e–h present that the Ni, P and C signals are evenly distributed on the surfaces of Ni2P, which also proves the Ni2P and CDs were successfully compounded.

Fig. 2 a evaluates the crystal of the samples by X-ray diffraction (XRD), indicating the Ni2P is also indexed to the hexagonal phase (PDF#3-953), where the diffraction peaks of hexagonal Ni2P at 2θ = 40.8°, 44.6°, 47.3°, 54.2° correspond respectively to the crystal planes are (111), (201), (210) and (300). As well as, the binary Ni2P/CDs composite only shows the diffraction peaks of Ni2P due to the low amount of CDs. In addition, Raman spectrum of CDs in Fig. S3 was applied to study the degree of graphitization of the samples. Obviously, from the peak intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the D band to the G band, it can be clearly seen that the CDs have a better graphitized structure. The components, electronic states and the interaction of Ni2P/CDs composite were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The existence of Ni, P and C elements in Ni2P/CDs composites have been confirmed by XPS full spectrum (Fig. S4). Fig. 2b–d provide the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni, P, C elements. In Fig. 2b, two peaks at 856.3 and 874.1 eV are accordance with Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 energy level, respectively. Meanwhile, the two other peaks at 861.1 and 879.8 eV belongs to the corresponding two satellite peaks. Furthermore, the peak at around 852.2 and 859.5 eV are attributed to the band of Ni-P [32,33]. For the P 2p, two peaks at 129.2 and 133.7 eV (Fig. 2c) are assigned to the Ni-P bonds in Ni2P and P-O, indicating the well connection of Ni2P and CDs [34]. Besides, three peaks at 288.4 eV, 285.6 eV and 284.3 eV in C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) could be ascribed to CO, C–O and C–C from CDs [35]. The prerequisite for photocatalyst to be able to decompose water is to have a suitable band gap, that is, the conduction band must be above the electronic potential E (H+/H2) meanwhile the valence band should be below the electronic potential E (O2/H2O). Therefore, the bandgap of Ni2P/CDs was measured. Firstly, the cycle voltammetry (CV) curve in Fig. 2e was used to calculate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) positions of Ni2P/CDs composite with ferrocene redox system as the external standard, and the detailed calculation is shown in supporting information [36]. Two pairs of redox peaks corresponded to ferrocene and catalyst can be seen clearly from the CV curve. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the catalyst can be calculated according to the formula to be −6.15 V and −4.16 V, respectively. Moreover, Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) is presented in Fig. S5. The ionization potential (equal to the valence band energy (eV)) can be calculated to −6.10 V by subtracting the width of the He I UPS spectrum from the exciting energy (21.22 eV), which is consistent with the CV results. Thus, as shown in Fig. 2f, the conduction band position of the catalyst is above H+/H2, and the valence band position is lower than O2/H2O and O2/H2O2, but it still cannot reach the oxidation level of H2O2/H2O. So, the catalyst has the ability of photocatalytic decomposition of water to generate H2 and O2, and can realize reduce O2 to generate H2O2. Meanwhile, UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. S6 display both Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs have favorable visible absorption.

The transient photovoltage measurement (TPV) tests of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs (Fig. 3 a) were performed to further insight into the catalytic mechanism under light excitation. Notably, Ni2P/CDs shows higher photovoltage intensity than that of Ni2P under the light excitation, indicating CDs promotes the increase of photogenerated charge, which is conducive to the occurrence of the catalytic reaction. Besides, TPV patterns of Ni2P with different CDs contents in Fig. S8a reveal NPC-20 can be excited to generate more charges than others catalysts. More carefully, the enlarged view of the tmax (the time required for peak intensity to reach a maximum) in TPV curves are displayed in Fig. 3b. Combined with Fig. S8b, it can be seen the addition of CDs increases the tmax value of the catalyst, but the content of CDs has little effect on tmax. The increase of tmax value is attributed to the electron storage property of CDs, and the electrons generated by Ni2P are quickly captured by CDs and then released slowly, thus stabilizing the photogenerated electrons. The enlarged view of the charge extraction process in TPV curves and the corresponding integral are shown in Fig. 3c. Theoretically, the integral area ratio represents the ratio of the ability of the catalyst to generate photoinduced electrons. It can be seen from Fig. 3c that the addition of CDs can make the catalyst excited to generate more photogenerated electrons. As well as, the enlarged view of the charge extraction process in TPV curves of Ni2P with different CDs contents is also provided in Fig. S8c. At the same time, the enlarged image of the decayed part in Fig. 3d shows that Ni2P/CDs composite has a lager charge relaxation time constant (τ) than that of Ni2P, suggesting Ni2P/CDs has a slower recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, which also proves that the characteristics of CDs to store charge and effectively separate photogenerated electrons and holes. As shown in Fig. S8d, although NPC-40 has the largest value of τ, implying that it has the largest electron storage capacity. Nevertheless, the excessive CDs hinders the absorption of light and reduce the generation of photogenerated electrons, limiting its catalytic activity. Based on the above test, it can be concluded that NPC-20 has higher catalytic activity in principle.

In order to explore the effect of different reaction conditions on the catalytic performance, the in-situ transient photovoltage (TPV) test (Fig. 4 a) was carried out. Obviously, Ni2P/CDs has the highest photovoltage intensity in N2 saturated anhydrous acetonitrile solution, where there is only charge excitation process and no electron depletion, because no reaction occurs. After adding trace water, with the occurrence of H2 production and O2 reduction reaction, electrons are gradually consumed and the photovoltage begins to decrease. Compared with the photovoltage generated in the water-bearing system, the influence of O2 on the photovoltage is more obvious, indicating that the amount of electrons consumed of O2 reduction in the catalytic system is larger than other reactions. The fitting curve of charge attenuation process in in-situ TPV curves of Ni2P/CDs in Fig. 4b shows the charge relaxation time constant (τ) under different test conditions. The value of τ is the largest under the condition of O2 saturation, reflecting that although the O2 reduction reaction consumes the most electrons, the O2 reduction reaction is not as fast as the H2 evolution reaction in terms of reaction kinetics. In Fig. 4c and d, the ratio of electron consumption under different conditions can be calculated by integrating the TPV curves. Here, the ratio of S1 and S2 represents that the ratio of the number of electrons consumed by the catalyst under water-saturated conditions and saturated O2 conditions, illustrating that although O2 reduction is slower in kinetics, the O2 reduction reaction is more advantageous when the photogenerated electron lifetime is long enough.

Next, we further carried out a kinetic analysis of this four-electron and two-electron cascade process, and the detailed analysis process is shown in the supporting information section. It can be seen that both H2 and H2O2 production, as a competitive reaction, need to consume electrons, while the O2 generated by water oxidation has little effect on the selectivity of H2O2. Therefore, the selectivity of H2O2 is related to dissolved O2 (Po2), pH value (H+), the number of electrons excited (e−). The more the dissolved O2 in water, the smaller the pH value, and the more electrons excited by light are conducive to the formation of H2O2. On the contrary, the less dissolved O2 in the water, the more favorable the production of H2.

The photocatalytic performance of different samples was measured through a multichannel photocatalytic reaction system and conducted in pure water under atmospheric pressure. As shown in Fig. 5 a, pure Ni2P can produce a small amount of H2 and H2O2 in water. With the addition of CDs, the catalytic activity is significantly improved, and the H2 and H2O2 yields of NPC-20 catalysts could reach 215.7 μmol/h/g and 1080.4 μmol/h/g, respectively. The variation trend of H2 and H2O2 yield over time in Fig. 6 b shows that no H2 and H2O2 were produced before light irradiation, while H2 and H2O2 production gradually increased with time within 8 h after light irradiation. In addition to the catalytic activity, stability is also an important factor in evaluating the catalytic performance, and the photocatalytic stability of Ni2P/CDs was detected by cyclic experiment. Cyclic experiment in Fig. 5c indicates that the Ni2P/CDs catalyst can maintain excellent catalytic activity after four cycles in air, revealing that the catalyst has good stability and anti-hydrogen peroxide corrosion ability. Furtherly, the stability of the Ni2P/CDs was confirmed by XRD and SEM images after reaction in Figs. S9 and S10. To investigate the effect of different atmospheres on the selectivity of H2 and H2O2, the performance of catalysts under saturated N2 and O2 atmosphere was also tested. The curve in Fig. 5d represents the effect of CDs on the yield ratio of H2 and H2O2. It can be seen that with the addition of CDs, the ratio of H2 and H2O2 increases gradually, reaches a maximum value and then decreases. This is due to the fact that the functional groups on the surface of the CDs facilitate the adsorption of protons, thereby increasing the efficiency of H2 production. In Fig. 5e, there is a definite increase in H2 production in the N2 saturated atmosphere, up to 258.6 μmol/h/g, but the H2O2 production decreased due to the reduction of O2. Conversely, H2O2 can reach 1281.4 μmol/h/g in O2-saturated water, but H2 production is markedly inhibited. The above results are highly consistent with in-situ TPV (Fig. 4a), which means H2 production and H2O2 production by O2 reduction are competitive reactions, and the ability of O2 reduction to consume electrons is stronger, that is to say, O2 has a significant selective effect on H2O2 production. As expected, QE in both O2 and N2 atmospheres correspondently correspond to UV–vis spectroscopy measurements (Fig. 5f and Fig. S11). According to the formula of supporting information part, the QE of Ni2P/CDs can reach 1.23% at 630 nm in O2 atmospheres, which suggests that the catalyst can effectively convert light energy into chemical energy in the visible light range. Moreover, a list for the related catalysts performance for comparison catalytic activities with bifunctional photocatalysts.

To further study the reaction mechanism of catalysis, we have carried out several electrochemical tests. Firstly, the photocurrent curves of Ni2P and Ni2P/CDs were collected under the open circuit potential to measure the photo-response characteristic. As shown in Fig. 6a, the photocurrent intensity of Ni2P/CDs sample is greatly improved compared with Ni2P under illumination, manifesting that the addition of CDs is conducive to the rapid separation of photo-generated charges, which also corresponds to the higher photocatalytic activity of Ni2P/CDs. In particular, the part marked by the blue frame in this figure shows that the photocurrent has a slow rising process under illumination, which indicates that the catalyst has charge storage characteristics. After the light is removed, the photocurrent decreases steadily to a stable level, which is mainly caused by the charge redistribution between the interfaces during the illumination process. In addition, the EIS Nyquist diagram depicted in Fig. 6b explore the charge transferability of the catalyst in ultrapure water. It can be seen that the circle radius of Ni2P/CDs is smaller than that of Ni2P, indicating that Ni2P/CDs has faster interface electron transfer speed. On the other hand, a faster electron transfer rate corresponds to a faster recombination rate of electron and hole, so it is not appropriate to explain the charge separation from the EIS Nyquist diagram alone. Furthermore, the rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) curves were utilized to characterize the electronic changes in the catalytic reaction. According to the formula mentioned in the supporting information, the number of transferred electrons measured under saturated (Fig. S12) N2 conditions was 3.62, which proves that the four-electron process of water oxidation to O2. Furthermore, the results of EPR experiments in Figs. S13 and S14 show that there are O2− active species under light, but no OH, demonstrating that H2O2 comes from the reduction reaction of O2. Many previous reports have proved that Ni2P has good catalytic activity in H2 evolution and O2 reduction reaction [37–41]. To further explore the active site of the catalytic reaction, the ORR LSV and HER LSV curves of Ni2P and CDs are displayed in Fig. 6c and d, which indicates that Ni2P has higher H2 evolution and O2 reduction activities.

As described in Fig. 7 , a proposed mechanism diagram is described in view of the above tests and analysis. Under visible light irradiation, Ni2P is easily excited to produce electrons and holes. However, the rapid recombination of photo-generated charges leads to low photocatalytic efficiency of the catalyst. With the addition of CDs, the photogenerated electrons generated can be easily captured by CDs, which accelerates the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus improving the catalytic activity. Meanwhile, the 4e−-2e− cascaded process can effectively avoid the deactivation of the catalyst due to H2O2 poisoning and improve the stability of the catalyst. In addition to the O2 produced by water oxidation, the O2 in the atmosphere also has a certain influence on the production of H2O2. To sum up, the catalyst can produce H2 and H2O2 simultaneously and controllably by adjusting the O2 content in the water.

In this work, we fabricated a Ni2P/CDs composite photocatalyst, which can produce H2 and H2O2 through a 4e−-2e− cascaded process at room temperature and pressure, and the yields of H2 and H2O2 can reach 258.6 μmol/h/g and 1281.4 μmol/h/g, respectively. Compared with Ni2P, the performance improvement of the Ni2P/CDs composite is mainly attributed to the characteristics of CDs, which can store and stabilize photogenerated electrons, improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes. The TPV and in-situ TPV measurements were applied to analyze the photocatalytic water-splitting on this Ni2P/CDs composite catalyst by measuring the maximum charge excitation ability and the photoinduced electron consumption of the catalyst under different reaction conditions. This work opens a new way for the high efficient photocatalyst design based on the regulation of the interface charge dynamics.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), National Key Research and Development Project of China (2020YFA0406104) Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province (2019B010933001), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the 111 Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.128184.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ bond modulation of graphitic carbon nitride to construct p-n homojunctions for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production
Adv. Funct. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway
Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, Y. Liu, N. Liu, Y. Han, X. Zhang, H. Huang, Y. Lifshitz, S.-T. Lee, J. Zhong, Z. Kang, Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway, Science. 347 (2015) 970–974.

Nat. Commun.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Noble-metal-free janus-like structures by cation exchange for Z-scheme photocatalytic water splitting under broadband light irradiation
